Evaluation of Addis Ababa school feeding program has been conducted. In the research, the effectiveness of SFP on educational outcomes relating to motivation to learn, enrollment in school, attentiveness and attendance, and educational attainment are discussed and the study showed the positive impact of School feeding program on enrollment rates and a negative impact on dropout rates. Dropout rates fell by 12% while overall enrollment increased by 15%.

Attendance, retention and enrollment of learners at primary level

Des études ont permis en substance de voir que les enfants issus des écoles à cantines sont plus réguliers à l'école. Le taux de réussite et la diplomation sont plus performants chez les enfants ayant bénéficié du programme d'alimentation. Les filles restent à l'école et fréquentent régulièrement les cours et sont assurées de la protection contre le mariage précoce, les grossesses non désirées et sont plus instruites sur les questions de la santé de la reproduction et du bien être de la femme. Les repas servis,de part leur qualité nutritionnelle et l'hygiène aux élèves, assurent aux élèves un bon développement cognitif et leur donne un avantage comparatif à l'acquisition des connaissance et même au développement physique.

Impacto en la memoria de corto plazo para los logros de aprendizaje en escolares del nivel primario que no viene tomando desayuno en casa. El servicio alimentario brindado permitió generar un ahorro no monetario en familias de estudiantes que participan en el programa.

The result of the Impact Evaluation Study on SBFP by the Phil. Institute for Development Studies (PIDS) showed improved attentiveness and sociability of SBFP beneficiaries. 

Based on the SBFP Evaluation and Feedback Survey,  the parents had an impression that their children became enthusiastic and energetic, gained weight after consuming the food commodities given, the body condition of the beneficiaries improved, and their immune system was strengthened.

The factor which adversely differentiates the school environment in terms of healthy nutrition and physical activity is primarily the school size, and then the school location. Systemic and social solutions should aim at reducing the small school “exclusion syndrome”, both in rural and in urban areas, also with regard to infrastructure and availability of 
conditions conducive to healthy nutrition and physical activity.
